subtitle: And the Mad Dog
summary: Pasteur refuses to be overshadowed by Koch's discoveries and charges into the fight against infectious disease, starting with child-bed fever. Though he is a chemist with no medical training, he recruits three young physician-assistants and begins a chaotic, multi-front assault on microbes of disease. Along the way, an accidental contamination in his lab sparks the idea that harmless bacteria might be used to fight deadly ones, planting the seed for his later work on vaccines.

segments.anchor_sentence: Do not think for a moment that Pasteur allowed his fame and name to be forgotten in the excitement kicked up by the sensational proofs of Koch that microbes murder men.

visual.scene_description: Pasteur dramatically interrupts a lecture at the Paris Academy of Medicine, charging up the aisle to the blackboard where he scrawls a chain of round microbes in chalk while astonished physicians look on.
visual.animation_prompt: Simple linocut print style illustration, bold black outlines, flat muted colors in dusty rose and sage green and ochre, rough textured edges, hand-printed look on natural paper, children's book illustration, a determined man with neat trimmed beard and round spectacles in formal black frock coat stands at a large blackboard drawing a chain of small circles in white chalk, one leg dragging slightly, rows of shocked 19th-century French physicians in dark suits recoil in their ornate wooden seats, grand Parisian lecture hall with tall arched windows and gas chandeliers

segments.anchor_sentence: Pasteur was in his late fifties now, but he was still as impetuous and enthusiastic as he had been at twenty-five.

visual.scene_description: Pasteur in his laboratory with his three young assistants Joubert, Roux, and Chamberland, who are teaching him to use a hypodermic syringe on a guinea pig while Pasteur watches squeamishly.
visual.animation_prompt: Simple linocut print style illustration, bold black outlines, flat muted colors in dusty rose and sage green and ochre, rough textured edges, hand-printed look on natural paper, children's book illustration, an older man with neat trimmed beard and round spectacles in black frock coat leans back squeamishly while three young men in rolled shirtsleeves demonstrate a hypodermic syringe on a small guinea pig atop a wooden laboratory bench, cluttered French laboratory with glass flasks and bottles on shelves, arched stone ceiling, oil lamp light

segments.anchor_sentence: Nothing is truer than that there is no one orthodox way of hunting microbes, and the differences between the ways Koch and Pasteur went at their work are the best illustrations of this.

visual.scene_description: Pasteur rushes between multiple experiments at once: examining a boil specimen under a microscope, visiting a hospital ward, and digging in a field looking for earthworms, capturing his frantic energy compared to Koch's calm precision.
visual.animation_prompt: Simple linocut print style illustration, bold black outlines, flat muted colors in dusty rose and sage green and ochre, rough textured edges, hand-printed look on natural paper, children's book illustration, a bearded man with round spectacles in disheveled black frock coat rushes between three scenes in a single composition: peering into a brass microscope at a cluttered bench, striding through a hospital corridor, and kneeling in a muddy French field examining earthworms, circular magnified inset showing chains of round cocci bacteria with visible cell walls

segments.anchor_sentence: To understand Pasteur, it is important to know his wild stabs and his failures as well as his triumphs.

visual.scene_description: Pasteur stares in surprise at a glass bottle of cloudy culture broth where contaminating airborne bacteria have choked out the anthrax bacilli, sparking his idea that harmless microbes could fight deadly ones.
visual.animation_prompt: Simple linocut print style illustration, bold black outlines, flat muted colors in dusty rose and sage green and ochre, rough textured edges, hand-printed look on natural paper, children's book illustration, a man with neat trimmed beard and round spectacles in black frock coat holds up a glass bottle of cloudy yellowish broth to the light with an expression of sudden realization, laboratory bench covered with sealed glass flasks and culture bottles, circular magnified inset showing large round harmless bacteria crowding out rod-shaped anthrax bacilli with visible endospores
